Architecture

Academic · Memorial Museum · Studio 7 · 2021

Gavai Museum

A memorial museum dedicated to Pandith Puttaraj Gavai and Pandit Panchakshari Gavai — the dedicated blind classical singers of Karnataka. The project explores performative and responsive architecture, with a form derived from the Chladni pattern. The design emphasises orientation, self-discovery and sensory experience, with accessibility for visually impaired visitors at its core.

MuseumMemorialResponsive Architecture Accessible DesignWaterscape10 Acres

Shading strategy — form twisted to achieve maximum shade throughout the day

Concept

Music as Form

The Chladni pattern — created when vibrations travel through a medium at a specific frequency — was the generative principle for the museum's form. The ideal frequency produces a predominantly circular and concentric pattern, creating an emphasis towards the centre, which becomes the ideal space for the memorial. The uniformity and symmetry of this pattern allows visually impaired users to map circulation more easily.

Raga — Flow

Free flowing movement as a characteristic of classical music. Visitors choose their path and explore the spaces — exploration becomes an important characteristic of the museum experience.

Tala — Intervals

Periodic intervals as a characteristic of classical music. The final circulation combines both factors, giving visitors a path to navigate with a sense of exploration remnant to create curiosity.

Accessibility

The ease of circulation was considered throughout form derivation. The design is accessible to the visually impaired — tactile paving, symmetrical layout, and sensory garden designed for blind users.

Masterplan & Site

Waterscape as Guide

The project site on the Sabarmati riverfront in Motera, Ahmedabad, has extreme solar radiation during summers. Waterscape was introduced as an important design decision — the plaza features 5 streams that meet at the centre, guiding visitors into the galleries. The form was also tweaked to achieve maximum shading throughout the day, with the building shading itself during early noon — prime time for visitors.

Masterplan — 10 acres, Motera, Ahmedabad · Shading analysis across early morning, late morning, early noon and late noon

Ground Floor Plan

Five Gallery Blocks + Auditorium

The museum is organised into an outer ring of amenities and temporary exhibits, an inner ring of music experience galleries and practice studios, and a core memorial that doubles as an open air theatre for special performances. Five outer blocks — entrance, souvenir shop, workshop and library, restaurant, and auditorium — surround the central gallery complex, each with its own plan logic responding to the Chladni geometry.

Ground floor plan — gallery blocks 1–5, auditorium, workshop & library, restaurant, souvenir shop · Individual gallery plans

Sections & Meta-Architecture

Trombe Wall & Jaali Openings

A Trombe wall integrated with jaali-like openings reduces the surface area for heat capture. These openings can be digitally generated based on solar shading to enhance the interior lighting of the galleries — a meta-architecture approach where building elements respond dynamically to environmental conditions. The clerestory windows allow light into the gallery while the inclined treatment maintains privacy and reduces solar gain.

Plan — galleries, memorial, waterscape · Section AA' · Cross section BB' · Sectional view · Meta-architecture — Trombe wall detail
